content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 507 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration • • • Executive Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest

(916) 122 5660                322 5901              322 6441              322 6444


January 6, 1978


Mr. Robert K. Booth
City Attorney
City of Palo Alto
Civic Center
250 Hamilton Avenue
Palo Alto, California 94301

Dear Mr. Booth:

     This is in response to your letter of December 30, 1977,
requesting advice pursuant to Government Code §87110(b).
Specifically, you ask whether a councilman may vote on a
resolution to acquire property from Stanford University by
eminent domain proceedings for construction of a road in an
assessment district. The councilman in question receives
funds from a partnership for the purpose of managing an office
building in the assessment district.

     The Political Reform Act provides in Government Code
§87100 and 87103 that a public official may not make or
participate in the making of a governmental decision which
will foreseeably and materially affect his financial interest.
The partnership is a source of income to the councilman
and, therefore, is a financial interest within the meaning of
the above cited Sections. Thus, the question is whether the
passage or defeat of the resolution will foreseeably and
materially affect the partnership. It is our understanding
that construction of the road has already been approved by
the city council. Moreover, if the property for the road
is not obtained by eminent domain, you have stipulated that
the property will be acquired from the University by purchase
or easement. If we assume, as maintained in your letter, that
the manner in which the Stanford property is acquired will
not foreseeably and materially affect the partnership, the
councilman may vote on the resolution. However, if it appears
that the decision on the manner of acquisition will have
material financial effect on the partnership because of, for
example, increased taxes or assessments, the councilman may
not vote.

Robert K. Booth
January 6, 1978
Page Two


     If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate
to call me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Kenneth W. Goshorn
                                        Research Specialist III
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

KWG:mfa

City of Palo Alto
CALIFORNIA

CIVIC CENTER
250 HAMILTON AVENUE
PALO ALTO
CALIFORNIA 94301
(415) 329-2171

December 30, 1977


Mr. Kenneth W. Goshorn
Research Specialist III
Conflicts of Interest Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Goshorn:

     This letter is a followup to our conversation of December 28,
1977. You will recall from previous conversations and corres-
pondence that a number of unusual conflicts questions have arisen
with respect to a road improvement project to be financed by an
assessment district. The current status of the project is as
follows:

     On December 19, 1977, the City Council approved formation of
the assessment district, and the project itself, including the
formula for spreading assessments to the various parcels involved
in the assessment district.

     One matter pending at the City Council meeting of December 19
did not receive approval and was continued to the City Council
meeting of January 9, 1978. That item is a resolution authorizing
eminent domain proceedings to acquire right of way from Stanford
University and a piece of property contiguous to the road which
will ultimately become a city park or will be held in open space.
That resolution requires a two-thirds vote of the City Council
(6 votes) and because one of the six Council members present and
voting on the matter on December 19 voted No, the resolution did
not pass at that time.

     Passage or failure of the resolution will not in any way
advance or prevent the project's progress. There is no actual
dispute as to the right of the City to acquire the land for the
project, nor any known dispute over the price thereof. The purpose
of the resolution is in order to preserve the integrity of
Stanford's trust over the lands which it holds. The University
views any voluntary sale or gift of its property as a possible
violation of the trust, and hence has requested a resolution of
eminent domain where its properties are to be acquired for public
projects. However, it feels it can grant easements without a
resolution of eminent domain without impairing the validity of
the trust. The project can be built on easements, if necessary.
The City desires, however, to acquire the fee interest in the

Mr. Kenneth W. Goshorn
12/30/77
Page two

properties in order to clear the title completely, and in order
to accommodate the desires of the University.

     One of the Council members who did not vote on December 19
has the following factual situation applicable to him.

     He is shown as owner-of-record of an office building located
within the assessment district; however, he holds said building
solely in a fiduciary relationship with numerous limited partners.
He does not share in any of the profits, losses, rentals, or in
any other way from operation of the building, nor does he possess
any interest that would entitle him to a share of the proceeds
if the building were sold.  The limited partners (and ultimately the
building's tenants) will pay the assessments.  It is my opinion
that he therefore does not have a direct or indirect interest
or interest within the meaning of Section 87103(a) or (b) of the
Government Code.  Nevertheless, because the assessments on the
property for the project as a whole would technically be his
responsibility, despite his full indemnity therefor provided by
contract from his limited partners, the Council member did not
participate in the formation of the assessment district.

     In addition to the foregoing "straw man" situation, the same
Council member holds a contract with the limited partners to
manage the building.  The contract is for a fixed term for a fixed
price, and none of the acts relating to this project or the
assessment district would have a material effect upon it.  His
personal remuneration from such contract is $375 a year, so he
does fall within the source of income test in Section 87103(c)
of the Government Code.

     The question we are seeking an answer to is whether or not
this Council member may participate in the vote on the resolution
of eminence domain on January 9, 1978.  We would like to emphasize
that passage or failure of the resolution will not affect the
project, the assessment district, his building, or his limited
partners, or the management contract under which he shares the
proceeds, in any significant respect, if at all.  The property
which is the subject of the resolution of eminent domain is not
any property or any portion thereof upon which is located the
office building.  The Council member's source of income is either
the management contract or the limited partners who in fact own
the office building.  They will not be affected at all, let alone
in any material respect, by this particular resolution, nor will
the tenants of the building, who, of course, pay a portion of
the cost of the project, be affected.  The decision to go forward
with the project has been made, as has the method of financing
and the amount to be assessed.

Mr. Kenneth W. Goshorn
12/30/77
Page three

     Accordingly, I would appreciate hearing the views of the
Commission conflicts staff in time to advise this Council member
for our meeting of January 9, 1978.  Please feel free to call
me if you need further information or details of what is a
very difficult situation to describe.  We very much appreciate
your help and assistance in dealing with this problem.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        [signature]
                                        ROBERT K. BOOTH, JR.
                                        City Attorney
RKB:vv

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city council member who has a fiduciary interest in an office building within an assessment district and a management contract with limited partners may participate in voting on a resolution authorizing eminent domain proceedings to acquire property from Stanford University for a road improvement project.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC response indicates that the council member should not participate in votes where his property or interests are directly affected, but the document appears incomplete and the full conclusion is not clearly legible in the provided text.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document quality is poor with significant illegibility in the header and footer sections. The incoming request letter from the City Attorney is clearly legible, but the FPPC's response appears fragmented and difficult to parse. The document appears to contain both the incoming request and a partial FPPC response, but the response portion is severely degraded. Classified as advice_letter with is_fppc_response=true based on document ID and context, though the actual FPPC response text is largely unreadable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a city council member who has a fiduciary interest in an office building within an assessment district and a management contract with limited partners may participate in voting on a resolution authorizing eminent domain proceedings to acquire property from Stanford University for a road improvement project.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC response indicates that the council member should not participate in votes where his property or interests are directly affected, but the document appears incomplete and the full conclusion is not clearly legible in the provided text.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from City Attorney Robert K. Booth, Jr. seeking FPPC guidance on whether a council member with a fiduciary interest in a building within an assessment district can vote on an eminent domain resolution, despite the resolution not directly affecting his property or management contract.
